title: Bronchiolitis — Assessment
oneLiner: Viral LRTI in infants <1 year; seasonal, self-limiting but can cause severe respiratory failure and apnoea.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory failure:** Grunting, head bobbing, severe recession, decreasing SpO₂ despite O₂, rising PCO₂ — **the infant who looks quiet but is exhausted** → need immediate escalation for NIV/CPAP.  
- **Apnoea:** Especially in infants <3 months, prematurity, or severe disease — **no audible cry, prolonged pause >20 seconds** → start stimulation, bag-mask ventilation, call anaesthetics/ICU.  
- **Sepsis (bacterial superinfection / meningitis):** High fever >39°C, toxic appearance, petechiae, poor perfusion despite adequate respiratory support — **consider septic screen + IV antibiotics** (do not assume all fever is viral).  
- **Foreign body aspiration:** Sudden onset, unilateral wheeze/crackles, no prodromal cough — **rigid bronchoscopy** if suspected; do not treat as bronchiolitis.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Coryza (runny nose, sneezing) for 2-3 days before LRTI signs.  
  – Cough (dry -> wet), wheeze/crackles.  
  – Low-grade fever (usually <39°C).  
  – Difficulty feeding (poor intake, tachypnoea prevents sucking).  
  – Irritability or lethargy.  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Increased work of breathing: tachypnoea, nasal flaring, intercostal/subcostal recession, tracheal tug.  
  – Prolonged expiration and fine inspiratory crackles (often called "wheeze" but actually crackles).  
  – Oxygen saturations <92% in air (if <90% persistent → severe).  
  – Signs of dehydration: dry mucous membranes, sunken fontanelle, reduced urine output.  
  – Palpable liver/spleen (hyperinflation pushes them down — not always pathological).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral-induced wheeze (older infants >1 year)** — recurrent episodes, personal/family history of atopy, responds to bronchodilators.  
- **Bacterial pneumonia** — high fever, focal dullness/bronchial breath sounds, toxic appearance.  
- **Foreign body aspiration** — abrupt onset, unilateral signs, typically in toddlers.  
- **Cardiac failure (e.g. VSD)** — poor feeding, hepatomegaly, gallop rhythm, murmur, cardiomegaly on CXR.  
- **Bronchiolitis obliterans / interstitial lung disease** — rare, prolonged course, no response to supportive care.

**Splitter:** “No response to salbutamol trial in an infant <1 year → bronchiolitis, not viral wheeze.”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Pulse oximetry (continuous if SpO₂ <94%).  
  – Respiratory rate, capillary refill, assessment of feeding ability.  
  – Suction nostrils with bulb syringe (can dramatically improve WOB if secretions are copious).  
- **Bloods:**  
  – Not routinely needed for mild-moderate cases.  
  – Consider if severe, toxic, or <3 months old: FBC, CRP, U&E, blood culture, blood gas (venous/capillary for pCO₂).  
- **Imaging:**  
  – **First-line:** None required for typical bronchiolitis.  
  – **Second-line (atypical features):** CXR (AP only) if: focal signs, no improvement, suspected foreign body, severe hypoxia not explained by clinical findings.  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Nasopharyngeal aspirate for RSV/other viruses (can reduce antibiotic use if positive, but not essential for management).  
  – No role for routine pertussis or blood cultures in mild cases.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE clinical diagnosis based on age, season, and typical features; no scoring system is universally used but some units use the **(modified) Yale Observation Scale** for severity.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Assessment of apnoea risk** — ask parents if they have witnessed any pauses in breathing; monitor with apnoea alarms in infants <6 weeks or those with risk factors (prematurity, congenital heart disease, neuromuscular disease).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – **Oxygen:** If SpO₂ <90% persistent, start via nasal cannulae (target 92-98% for severe; 94-98% for all).  
  – **Suction:** Gentle nasal suction before feeds if secretions are blocking nostrils.  
  – **Fluids:** If not feeding well, place NG tube (if still able to tolerate oral) or IV fluids (0.45% or 0.9% sodium chloride + 5% glucose) guided by hydration status.  
  – **No bronchodilators** (salbutamol, ipratropium) or steroids — contrary to older practice; NICE advises against routine use.  
  – **No antibiotics** unless there is clear evidence of bacterial co-infection (focal consolidation, high CRP, toxic).  
  – **Trial of salbutamol** is only considered in children >1 year with a past history of wheeze — stop if no improvement after 2 doses.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Continue oxygen weaning as tolerated; O₂ can be delivered via low-flow nasal cannulae or headbox.  
  – Support feeding: NG feeds if oral intake <50% of usual.  
  – Suction as needed, especially before feeds and sleep.  
  – Respiratory physiotherapy is not beneficial (NICE).  
  – Monitor for apnoea, especially in infants <3 months or with risk factors.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Most infants recover fully; follow-up with GP or health visitor.  
  – Palivizumab prophylaxis is reserved for high-risk infants (premature, CHD, CLD) — given monthly during RSV season, but this is a preventive measure, not treatment.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – **Fluids:** IV or NG: 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose at maintenance rate (e.g. 100-150 mL/kg/day depending on age and hydration).  
  – **VTE prophylaxis:** Not relevant for infants.  
  – **Diet:** Encourage breastmilk/formula; if poor intake, NG or IV; do not NBM unless there is concern for aspiration.  
  – **Observation frequency:**  
    - Mild: 4 hourly (SpO₂, resp rate, work of breathing, feeding).  
    - Moderate: 1-2 hourly for the first 24h, then 4 hourly if stable.  
    - Severe/ICU: continuous monitoring (SpO₂, HR, RR, invasive BP, capnography if ventilated).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:** No specific antiviral (ribavirin not used in UK for normal hosts; palivizumab is prophylaxis only).  
- **Supportive:**  
  – **Paracetamol** (if fever >38.5°C causing distress): 15 mg/kg/dose orally or IV 4-6 hourly PRN, max 60 mg/kg/day (max 4 doses in 24h).  
  – **Ibuprofen** (if paracetamol inadequate, ensure child >3 months and adequate hydration): 5-10 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PRN, max 30 mg/kg/day.  
- **Common alternatives:** Nil specific.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Ibuprofen: avoid if dehydrated, bleeding risk, asthma (rare in infants).  
  – Paracetamol: avoid in severe hepatic impairment.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Check local policy for doses in renal/hepatic impairment; paracetamol reduced frequency in severe liver disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Paracetamol overdose** — double-check weight-based dose; in infants, 10x dosing errors occur. Use a syringe, not a spoon, and document weight immediately.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  – Apnoea (especially in young/premature infants).  
  – Respiratory failure requiring NIV/intubation.  
  – Dehydration and electrolyte disturbances (hyponatraemia from hypotonic IV fluids).  
  – Secondary bacterial pneumonia (especially *Streptococcus pneumoniae*, *Staphylococcus aureus*).  
  – Pneumothorax (rare, usually from aggressive ventilation).  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Increased risk of recurrent wheeze and asthma in later childhood (causal link unclear).  
  – Bronchiectasis (rare, usually only in severe RSV with pre-existing lung disease).  
  – Post-bronchiolitis obliterans (extremely rare; usually adenovirus-related).
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** <1% in healthy term infants in the UK; higher in those with congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease, immunodeficiency (up to 10-20%).  
- **Recovery:** Symptoms peak at day 3-5, cough may persist for 2-3 weeks.  
- **Chronicity:** No specific chronic sequelae in most; some develop wheeze but not true COPD.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a trial of nasal suction** before escalating respiratory support – a single, gentle suction can dramatically reduce work of breathing if the nose is blocked.  
- **Reassess every 30-60 minutes** in the first few hours of admission – infants can deteriorate from mild to severe in an hour.  
- **Check feeding ability** not just saturations – an infant who cannot feed needs admission even if saturations >94%.  
- **Use a saturations target range** (e.g., 94-98% for those on O₂) – avoid hypoxaemia but also avoid hyperoxia, which is harmful in premature infants.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not routinely prescribe bronchodilators** – they are ineffective and can cause tachycardia, jitteriness, and vomiting.  
- **Do not give systemic steroids** – no benefit in standard bronchiolitis, even if there is a wheeze component.  
- **Do not start antibiotics without evidence of bacterial infection** – they are not indicated for viral bronchiolitis and contribute to resistance.  
- **Do not send a CXR for every infant** – it rarely changes management and exposes to radiation; only if atypical features or severe disease.  
- **Do not underestimate severity in infants <3 months** – they often present with apnoea rather than respiratory distress; monitor these patients more closely.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  – Worsening difficulty breathing (e.g., grunting, head bobbing, not feeding).  
  – Apnoeic episode (stopped breathing for >20 seconds or turned blue/pale).  
  – Signs of dehydration (no wet nappy for >6 hours, dry mouth, sunken eyes, crying without tears).  
  – Fever >38.5°C for >5 days or if the child becomes lethargic/irritable.  
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 48-72 hours if mild; health visitor can monitor feeding and weight.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / anaesthetics / ICU if:**  
  – SpO₂ <85% despite high-flow O₂ (or FiO₂ >0.6).  
  – Apnoea with desaturation requiring stimulation or bag-mask ventilation.  
  – Deteriorating consciousness (lethargic, unresponsive).  
  – Rising PCO₂ on blood gas (>6.5 kPa in a patient with severe distress).  
  – Need for ventilatory support (CPAP or intubation) – **do not wait for full arrest**.  
- **Use local paediatric early warning score (PEWS)** – trigger if PEWS exceeds threshold, or if any single parameter is red (e.g., SpO₂ <85%).  
- **Specific threshold for escalation:**  
  – Any infant with a respiratory rate >70/min, heart rate >200/min, or oxygen requirement >2L/min for age → discuss with paediatric registrar.  
  – Infants with known risk factors (prematurity, CHD, CLD, neuromuscular) need a lower threshold for escalation.

rawMarkdown:
# Bronchiolitis — Assessment

**One-liner:** Viral LRTI in infants <1 year; seasonal, self-limiting but can cause severe respiratory failure and apnoea.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory failure:** Grunting, head bobbing, severe recession, decreasing SpO₂ despite O₂, rising PCO₂ — **the infant who looks quiet but is exhausted** → need immediate escalation for NIV/CPAP.  
- **Apnoea:** Especially in infants <3 months, prematurity, or severe disease — **no audible cry, prolonged pause >20 seconds** → start stimulation, bag-mask ventilation, call anaesthetics/ICU.  
- **Sepsis (bacterial superinfection / meningitis):** High fever >39°C, toxic appearance, petechiae, poor perfusion despite adequate respiratory support — **consider septic screen + IV antibiotics** (do not assume all fever is viral).  
- **Foreign body aspiration:** Sudden onset, unilateral wheeze/crackles, no prodromal cough — **rigid bronchoscopy** if suspected; do not treat as bronchiolitis.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Coryza (runny nose, sneezing) for 2-3 days before LRTI signs.  
  – Cough (dry -> wet), wheeze/crackles.  
  – Low-grade fever (usually <39°C).  
  – Difficulty feeding (poor intake, tachypnoea prevents sucking).  
  – Irritability or lethargy.  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Increased work of breathing: tachypnoea, nasal flaring, intercostal/subcostal recession, tracheal tug.  
  – Prolonged expiration and fine inspiratory crackles (often called "wheeze" but actually crackles).  
  – Oxygen saturations <92% in air (if <90% persistent → severe).  
  – Signs of dehydration: dry mucous membranes, sunken fontanelle, reduced urine output.  
  – Palpable liver/spleen (hyperinflation pushes them down — not always pathological).  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral-induced wheeze (older infants >1 year)** — recurrent episodes, personal/family history of atopy, responds to bronchodilators.  
- **Bacterial pneumonia** — high fever, focal dullness/bronchial breath sounds, toxic appearance.  
- **Foreign body aspiration** — abrupt onset, unilateral signs, typically in toddlers.  
- **Cardiac failure (e.g. VSD)** — poor feeding, hepatomegaly, gallop rhythm, murmur, cardiomegaly on CXR.  
- **Bronchiolitis obliterans / interstitial lung disease** — rare, prolonged course, no response to supportive care.

**Splitter:** “No response to salbutamol trial in an infant <1 year → bronchiolitis, not viral wheeze.”

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Pulse oximetry (continuous if SpO₂ <94%).  
  – Respiratory rate, capillary refill, assessment of feeding ability.  
  – Suction nostrils with bulb syringe (can dramatically improve WOB if secretions are copious).  
- **Bloods:**  
  – Not routinely needed for mild-moderate cases.  
  – Consider if severe, toxic, or <3 months old: FBC, CRP, U&E, blood culture, blood gas (venous/capillary for pCO₂).  
- **Imaging:**  
  – **First-line:** None required for typical bronchiolitis.  
  – **Second-line (atypical features):** CXR (AP only) if: focal signs, no improvement, suspected foreign body, severe hypoxia not explained by clinical findings.  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Nasopharyngeal aspirate for RSV/other viruses (can reduce antibiotic use if positive, but not essential for management).  
  – No role for routine pertussis or blood cultures in mild cases.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** NICE clinical diagnosis based on age, season, and typical features; no scoring system is universally used but some units use the **(modified) Yale Observation Scale** for severity.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Assessment of apnoea risk** — ask parents if they have witnessed any pauses in breathing; monitor with apnoea alarms in infants <6 weeks or those with risk factors (prematurity, congenital heart disease, neuromuscular disease).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – **Oxygen:** If SpO₂ <90% persistent, start via nasal cannulae (target 92-98% for severe; 94-98% for all).  
  – **Suction:** Gentle nasal suction before feeds if secretions are blocking nostrils.  
  – **Fluids:** If not feeding well, place NG tube (if still able to tolerate oral) or IV fluids (0.45% or 0.9% sodium chloride + 5% glucose) guided by hydration status.  
  – **No bronchodilators** (salbutamol, ipratropium) or steroids — contrary to older practice; NICE advises against routine use.  
  – **No antibiotics** unless there is clear evidence of bacterial co-infection (focal consolidation, high CRP, toxic).  
  – **Trial of salbutamol** is only considered in children >1 year with a past history of wheeze — stop if no improvement after 2 doses.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Continue oxygen weaning as tolerated; O₂ can be delivered via low-flow nasal cannulae or headbox.  
  – Support feeding: NG feeds if oral intake <50% of usual.  
  – Suction as needed, especially before feeds and sleep.  
  – Respiratory physiotherapy is not beneficial (NICE).  
  – Monitor for apnoea, especially in infants <3 months or with risk factors.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Most infants recover fully; follow-up with GP or health visitor.  
  – Palivizumab prophylaxis is reserved for high-risk infants (premature, CHD, CLD) — given monthly during RSV season, but this is a preventive measure, not treatment.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – **Fluids:** IV or NG: 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose at maintenance rate (e.g. 100-150 mL/kg/day depending on age and hydration).  
  – **VTE prophylaxis:** Not relevant for infants.  
  – **Diet:** Encourage breastmilk/formula; if poor intake, NG or IV; do not NBM unless there is concern for aspiration.  
  – **Observation frequency:**  
    - Mild: 4 hourly (SpO₂, resp rate, work of breathing, feeding).  
    - Moderate: 1-2 hourly for the first 24h, then 4 hourly if stable.  
    - Severe/ICU: continuous monitoring (SpO₂, HR, RR, invasive BP, capnography if ventilated).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:** No specific antiviral (ribavirin not used in UK for normal hosts; palivizumab is prophylaxis only).  
- **Supportive:**  
  – **Paracetamol** (if fever >38.5°C causing distress): 15 mg/kg/dose orally or IV 4-6 hourly PRN, max 60 mg/kg/day (max 4 doses in 24h).  
  – **Ibuprofen** (if paracetamol inadequate, ensure child >3 months and adequate hydration): 5-10 mg/kg/dose 6-8 hourly PRN, max 30 mg/kg/day.  
- **Common alternatives:** Nil specific.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Ibuprofen: avoid if dehydrated, bleeding risk, asthma (rare in infants).  
  – Paracetamol: avoid in severe hepatic impairment.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Check local policy for doses in renal/hepatic impairment; paracetamol reduced frequency in severe liver disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Paracetamol overdose** — double-check weight-based dose; in infants, 10x dosing errors occur. Use a syringe, not a spoon, and document weight immediately.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  – Apnoea (especially in young/premature infants).  
  – Respiratory failure requiring NIV/intubation.  
  – Dehydration and electrolyte disturbances (hyponatraemia from hypotonic IV fluids).  
  – Secondary bacterial pneumonia (especially *Streptococcus pneumoniae*, *Staphylococcus aureus*).  
  – Pneumothorax (rare, usually from aggressive ventilation).  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Increased risk of recurrent wheeze and asthma in later childhood (causal link unclear).  
  – Bronchiectasis (rare, usually only in severe RSV with pre-existing lung disease).  
  – Post-bronchiolitis obliterans (extremely rare; usually adenovirus-related).

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** <1% in healthy term infants in the UK; higher in those with congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease, immunodeficiency (up to 10-20%).  
- **Recovery:** Symptoms peak at day 3-5, cough may persist for 2-3 weeks.  
- **Chronicity:** No specific chronic sequelae in most; some develop wheeze but not true COPD.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a trial of nasal suction** before escalating respiratory support – a single, gentle suction can dramatically reduce work of breathing if the nose is blocked.  
- **Reassess every 30-60 minutes** in the first few hours of admission – infants can deteriorate from mild to severe in an hour.  
- **Check feeding ability** not just saturations – an infant who cannot feed needs admission even if saturations >94%.  
- **Use a saturations target range** (e.g., 94-98% for those on O₂) – avoid hypoxaemia but also avoid hyperoxia, which is harmful in premature infants.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not routinely prescribe bronchodilators** – they are ineffective and can cause tachycardia, jitteriness, and vomiting.  
- **Do not give systemic steroids** – no benefit in standard bronchiolitis, even if there is a wheeze component.  
- **Do not start antibiotics without evidence of bacterial infection** – they are not indicated for viral bronchiolitis and contribute to resistance.  
- **Do not send a CXR for every infant** – it rarely changes management and exposes to radiation; only if atypical features or severe disease.  
- **Do not underestimate severity in infants <3 months** – they often present with apnoea rather than respiratory distress; monitor these patients more closely.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  – Worsening difficulty breathing (e.g., grunting, head bobbing, not feeding).  
  – Apnoeic episode (stopped breathing for >20 seconds or turned blue/pale).  
  – Signs of dehydration (no wet nappy for >6 hours, dry mouth, sunken eyes, crying without tears).  
  – Fever >38.5°C for >5 days or if the child becomes lethargic/irritable.  
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 48-72 hours if mild; health visitor can monitor feeding and weight.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / anaesthetics / ICU if:**  
  – SpO₂ <85% despite high-flow O₂ (or FiO₂ >0.6).  
  – Apnoea with desaturation requiring stimulation or bag-mask ventilation.  
  – Deteriorating consciousness (lethargic, unresponsive).  
  – Rising PCO₂ on blood gas (>6.5 kPa in a patient with severe distress).  
  – Need for ventilatory support (CPAP or intubation) – **do not wait for full arrest**.  
- **Use local paediatric early warning score (PEWS)** – trigger if PEWS exceeds threshold, or if any single parameter is red (e.g., SpO₂ <85%).  
- **Specific threshold for escalation:**  
  – Any infant with a respiratory rate >70/min, heart rate >200/min, or oxygen requirement >2L/min for age → discuss with paediatric registrar.  
  – Infants with known risk factors (prematurity, CHD, CLD, neuromuscular) need a lower threshold for escalation.
